subject: Mathematics
strand_rationale: The unit on Numbers is the basis of all mathematics. Number is used for order and size in calculations. We calculate by adding, subtracting, multiplying, and dividing numbers, either mentally, written or by using technology. We also build patterns using numbers in written or symbolic forms to represent mathematical ideas. We need numbers to count, compare, and measure. In everyday life, we use numbers for almost everything. Our age, weight and height, time, money, and work are measured using numbers. Numbers have meaning and are represented in many different forms. This strand covers whole numbers, decimal, fractions, percentages, integers, scientific notation, exponential numbers, and logarithmic numbers under the Real Numbers System.
content_standard: Students will be able to represent numbers in various situations and forms, develop fluency in calculations through operations, use base ten as key for extending numbers and operations, and apply numbers in practical situations to develop number sense.
benchmark: Use and represent factors, multiples, primes, composites, in various forms and situations.
evidence_outcomes: Use and represent factors, multiples, primes, composites, in various forms and situations.
Calculate fractions, decimals and percentages and their conversions.
Extend the properties of exponents to rational exponents.
Use properties of rational and irrational numbers.
Apply rules of estimation to solve authentic situations.
Use appropriate rules and various representations of directed numbers and mixed operations to solve authentic problems.
Solve problems with squares and square roots, limited to square roots of numbers.
Use ratios, proportions and percent to represent the relationship between two quantities and solve problems.
Add, subtract, multiply, and divide numbers with positive and negative exponents.
Use index notation with numbers to establish the index laws with positive integral indices and the zero index.

subject: Mathematics
strand_rationale: Topics of geometry, measurement, and transformation remain important component of the mathematics curriculum at all levels. This body of knowledge helps in making informed decisions on some necessities in life. We think geometrically when we choose shapes, and solids, we want precision so we take measurements seriously, and we like to be creative so we transform what is common to something new. When questioned we justify our choices, for clarity we explain the processes required to arrive at solutions. These knowledge, skills, and techniques are integrated in teaching of these topics. In PNG context, building a house is almost every family’s dream. Shape, measurement and transformation come at a cost. A sound mathematical knowledge will ensure satisfaction at an acceptable cost.
content_standard: Students will be able to comprehend the meaning and significant of geometry, measurements and spatial relationship including units and system of measurement and develop and use techniques, tools, and formulas for measuring the properties of objects and relationships among the properties and use transformations and symmetry to analyze mathematical situations.
benchmark: Apply metric systems of measurements to solve problems.
evidence_outcomes: Solve rate values represented on tables and graphs.
Apply metric system of measurement to solve problems in area, volume and rates.
Apply appropriate formula and units to calculate perimeter and areas of various plane figures and composite shapes.
Apply appropriate formula and units to calculate surface area and volume of prisms, cylinders, pyramids, right cones, spheres and related composite solids.
Perform transformations including reflection, rotation and translation and describe the size, position and orientation of the resulting shapes.
Explain the concepts of parallel lines, perpendicular lines, angles, and angle sum of polygons.
Use the enlargement transformation and the conditions for triangles to explain similarities and solve problems.
Explain angles formed by chords and tangent of a circle.

content_standard: Students will be able to comprehend the meaning and significant of geometry, measurements and spatial relationship including units and system of measurement and develop and use techniques, tools, and formulas for measuring the properties of objects and relationships among the properties and use transformations and symmetry to analyze mathematical situations.
benchmark: Use the enlargement transformation and the conditions for triangles to explain similarities and solve problems.
evidence_outcomes: Solve rate values represented on tables and graphs.
Apply metric system of measurement to solve problems in area, volume and rates.
Apply appropriate formula and units to calculate perimeter and areas of various plane figures and composite shapes.
Apply appropriate formula and units to calculate surface area and volume of prisms, cylinders, pyramids, right cones, spheres and related composite solids.
Perform transformations including reflection, rotation and translation and describe the size, position and orientation of the resulting shapes.
Explain the concepts of parallel lines, perpendicular lines, angles, and angle sum of polygons.
Use the enlargement transformation and the conditions for triangles to explain similarities and solve problems.
Explain angles formed by chords and tangent of a circle.

subject: Mathematics
strand_rationale: Algebra is the language of generalization. If something is done once you probably don’t need algebra. But if the process is to be done repeatedly, algebra provides a very simple language for describing what is being done. Algebra is the language through which we describe patterns. Many areas of mathematics integrate algebra specifically for problem solving. When we add, subtract, multiply, and divide we follow patterns. Algebra develops patterns into equations and formulas that come handy in life. You may want to buy a piece of land and want to know the formula for area, or sew clothes and want to know the amount of fabric needed or perimeter for amount of fencing etc... They all involves applying some formula.
content_standard: Students will be able to interpret various types of patterns and functional relationships, use symbolic forms to represent, model, and analyze mathematical situations and collect, organize, and represent data to answer questions.
benchmark: Determine the slope and equation of a line when given the graph of a line, two points on the line, or the equation of the line.
evidence_outcomes: Solve linear equations and inequalities with two variables using algebraic methods, manipulatives or models.
Determine the slope of a line when given the graph of a line, two points on the line, or the equation of the line.

Use a variety of strategies including concrete modelling, pictorial representation, and algebraic manipulation to add, subtract, multiply, divide and factor first and second-degree binomials and trinomials in one variable.
Apply distributive law to the expansion of algebraic expressions, including binomials, and collect like terms where appropriate.
Solve linear simultaneous equations using algebraic and graphical techniques including use of digital technology.
Solve various problems on number patterns.
Factorize monic and non-monic quadratic expressions and solve a wide range of quadratic equations derived from a variety of contexts.
Apply the four operations to simple algebraic fractions with numerical denominators.

subject: Mathematics
strand_rationale: In the study of social science, students might encounter data. In a science laboratory experience, students might collect data. In a mathematics classroom, students would be expected to know how to calculate the mean of a set of numbers. Statistics has become more important, its connections with everyday literacy, science, health, and the social sciences suggest statistics must be taught across the curriculum as a topic in mathematics. Probability is required for reading newspapers, being an informed citizen, it helps one to understand and evaluate information in the world around us, and it is a prerequisite knowledge for other fields of study. In the current curriculum design, students may encounter probability topics, by themselves or in connection with descriptive statistics as early as elementary level, or as late as senior high school.
content_standard: Students will be able to investigate how to interpret data using methods of exploratory data analysis, develop and evaluate inferences, predictions and arguments that are based on data and understand and apply basic notions of chance and probability.
benchmark: Analyse and interpret data using mean, median, mode, range and frequency.
evidence_outcomes: Analyse and interpret data using mean, median, mode, range and frequency.
Design a study, collect data, and select the appropriate representation to make conclusions and generalizations.
Judge the validity of reported data, conclusions and generalizations.
Calculate probabilities for simple events under different relationships, including independent, dependent, with replacement and without replacement.
Identify complementary events and use the sum of probabilities to solve problems.
Describe events using language of ‘at least’, exclusive ‘or’, inclusive ‘or’, and ‘and’.

Statistics has become more important, its connections with everyday literacy, science, health, and the social sciences suggest statistics must be taught across the curriculum as a topic in mathematics. Probability is required for reading newspapers, being an informed citizen, it helps one to understand and evaluate information in the world around us, and it is a prerequisite knowledge for other fields of study. In the current curriculum design, students may encounter probability topics, by themselves or in connection with descriptive statistics as early as elementary level, or as late as senior high school.
content_standard: Students will be able to investigate how to interpret data using methods of exploratory data analysis, develop and evaluate inferences, predictions and arguments that are based on data and understand and apply basic notions of chance and probability.
benchmark: Compute probabilities using appropriate methods such as lists and tree diagrams or through experimental or simulation activities.
evidence_outcomes: Analyse and interpret data using mean, median, mode, range and frequency.
Design a study, collect data, and select the appropriate representation to make conclusions and generalizations.
Judge the validity of reported data, conclusions and generalizations.
Calculate probabilities for simple events under different relationships, including independent, dependent, with replacement and without replacement.
Identify complementary events and use the sum of probabilities to solve problems.
Describe events using language of ‘at least’, exclusive ‘or’, inclusive ‘or’, and ‘and’.
